Class HtmlLoader

java.lang.Object
servlets.HtmlLoader
All Implemented Interfaces:
Servlet
public class HtmlLoader extends Object implements Servlet
Servlet that serves static files from a directory under a registered URI prefix.

Typically registered as server.addServlet("GET", "/app/", new HtmlLoader("html_files")). A request to GET /app/index.html resolves to html_files/index.html relative to the configured base directory.

Path resolution: The first URI segment (the servlet prefix, e.g. "app") is skipped; remaining segments form the relative file path. If no sub-path is present, index.html is served by default. Files are first looked up relative to baseDir, then relative to user.dir/baseDir as a fallback.

Content types: Determined by file extension — HTML, CSS, JavaScript, PNG, JPEG, GIF, and plain text are supported. Missing files or directories yield a 404 Not Found HTML error page.

Thread safety: This servlet is stateless after construction (the baseDir field is final) and safe for concurrent reads of the filesystem.

See Also:

  • HTTPServer.addServlet(java.lang.String, java.lang.String, servlets.Servlet)

  • Constructor Details

    • HtmlLoader

      public HtmlLoader(String baseDir)
      Creates a static-file servlet rooted at the given directory.
      Parameters:
      baseDir - the root directory from which static assets are served

  • Method Details

    • handle

      public void handle(RequestParser.RequestInfo ri, OutputStream toClient) throws IOException
      Resolves the requested file from URI segments and writes an HTTP response.

      Returns 200 OK with the appropriate Content-Type when the file exists, or 404 Not Found with a styled HTML error page otherwise.

      Specified by:
      handle in interface Servlet
      Parameters:
      ri - the parsed request; RequestParser.RequestInfo.getUriSegments() is used to determine the relative file path
      toClient - the client output stream for the HTTP response
      Throws:
      IOException - if an I/O error occurs while reading the file or writing the response

    • close

      public void close() throws IOException
      Releases servlet resources.

      Currently a no-op; no persistent resources are held between requests.

      Specified by:
      close in interface Servlet
      Throws:
      IOException - if an I/O error occurs during cleanup
